title: Osmosis Across a Membrane
description: Watch water molecules move from high to low concentration across a semi-permeable membrane — the force that drives all of cell biology.

concentration_left.label: Left Concentration
concentration_left.description: Solute concentration on the left side (more solute = less water)

concentration_right.label: Right Concentration
concentration_right.description: Solute concentration on the right side

narration.text: Blue = water molecules. Yellow = solute (e.g. salt). The membrane in the middle lets water through but blocks solute.
Water moves by random motion — but there are more water molecules on the left (less solute), so more cross to the right by sheer probability.
Osmosis is not 'attracted' by anything. Water doesn't 'want' to move. It's purely statistical: more molecules on one side means more crossings per second.
As water moves right, the right side swells. This is osmotic pressure — the force driving water into cells, inflating plant cells, and governing kidney function.
Equilibrium approaches. The concentrations equalise. Water still crosses in both directions — but at equal rates. Net movement stops.
Every cell in your body uses osmosis. Dehydration, IV drips, and food preservation all work by manipulating this concentration difference.
